fields.definition_origin: Popularized by Cal AI's onboarding (Henry Langmack & Zach Yadegari, 2024) — the team placed Apple's `SKStoreReviewController.requestReview()` call AFTER the AI plan reveal but BEFORE the paywall. The pattern was reverse-engineered and propagated by Adam Lyttle (X teardown 2024), Cesar Alvarez (Cal AI breakdown thread Sep 2024), and the Mobbin Cal AI flow capture. The underlying psychology is Kahneman's Peak-End Rule (1993) operationalized for the App Store review nudge.
fields.core_mechanism: App Store review prompts collect the highest-rated reviews when fired at the user's emotional peak. Mid-onboarding (post-AI-reveal, pre-paywall) is engineered to BE the peak — the user has just seen a personalized plan with their own goals stitched in, dopamine is high, sunk cost is high (they invested 3-10 minutes), and the paywall hasn't yet introduced the negative affect of being asked for money. Firing the prompt here harvests the peak before the paywall potentially sours it. Apple's API is rate-limited to 3 prompts per 365 days, so spending one during the engineered peak yields better star averages than letting it fire opportunistically mid-app where typical reviews complain about bugs.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: Mesolimbic dopamine peak from the AI-reveal payoff; positive valence in the orbitofrontal cortex creates a halo that raises rating valence. Pairs with peak-end memory consolidation — the prompt itself becomes part of the encoded peak. [uncertain on direct fMRI evidence for app review timing]
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) Cal AI grew to ~$30M ARR Year 1 with a 4.8★ App Store average across 100k+ ratings (App Store stats) — credited by founders in interviews to mid-onboarding placement. (2) Adam Lyttle (X teardowns 2024) reports 4-8× more rating volume with mid-onboarding placement vs. random in-app placement; sample drawn from his portfolio of utility apps. (3) Apple's WWDC 2017 'Engineering for App Store' guidance recommends firing requestReview at 'a logical point of value', which the App Store team has reiterated. (4) Adapty 2026 high-performing paywall report notes top apps cluster reviews into 'engineered peak' moments. (5) Anecdotally widespread in 2024-2025 wellness onboarding (Cal AI, Rise Sleep, Plant Daddy, Calorie Mama).
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: App Store rating count, average star rating, review-prompt-shown-to-rating-submitted ratio (Apple does not expose true conversion but proxy via rating count delta vs. install delta), review sentiment NPS.
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Adam Lyttle reports 4-8× rating volume vs. random placement (practitioner case study, sample tens of utility apps). Cal AI's 4.8★ at 100k+ ratings is best-in-class for the wellness category. Both unverified by independent benchmark.
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) iOS: call `SKStoreReviewController.requestReview(in: windowScene)` (iOS 14+) or the `requestReview` SwiftUI environment value, AFTER the AI plan reveal animation completes and BEFORE the paywall navigation push. Apple's system rate-limits to 3/year — never call manually beyond that. (2) Android: use Google Play In-App Review API (`ReviewManager.requestReviewFlow()` then `launchReviewFlow()`) — Play's quota is opaque. (3) React Native: `expo-store-review` (`StoreReview.requestReview()`) wraps both. (4) Gate by completion: only fire if the user completed all prior onboarding steps AND has been in-app long enough that Apple won't suppress (Apple's heuristic — typically requires user to have used app for some session time; mid-onboarding is acceptable per WWDC guidance). (5) Detect availability before showing UI — `StoreReview.isAvailableAsync()`. (6) Telemetry — emit `review_prompt_shown` and (if API confirms) `review_prompt_completed`; also track rating delta to confirm the placement is producing more ratings. (7) Cache locally that the prompt was shown so you never re-show (even though Apple suppresses, the user may see your custom 'rate us' affordance you should also suppress).
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Cal AI step 31 of 33 — immediately after AI macro plan reveal animation, before paywall slide-in. (2) Rise Sleep — fires after the personalized sleep schedule reveal, before the trial-offer screen. (3) Plant Daddy — fires after the AI plant identification reveal during onboarding, before the upsell. (4) Calorie Mama — fires after the first photo-to-calorie demo within onboarding. (5) Pi Network onboarding (less subtle): fires immediately after account creation (premature). (6) Gentler Streak (Apple Design Award 2022 anecdote — fires after first activity sync, before notification permission).
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile-only pattern. App Store / Play Store review APIs do not have a web equivalent; web apps gather reviews via Trustpilot/G2 prompts asynchronously by email. The mobile peak harvest is unique to the mobile app stores.
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: Apple's review prompt UI is system-rendered (translucent overlay, native-styled stars) — ships in Liquid Glass on iOS 26+ automatically. Material 3 Expressive — Google Play's in-app review uses a system-rendered modal that matches M3E. The pattern itself is OS-conformant; you cannot customize the prompt chrome.
fields.category_fit: Strongest fit: wellness/habit (Cal AI, Rise, Headspace), AI-wrapper (Plant Daddy, Calorie Mama), edtech (Headway), fitness, journaling. Acceptable fit: utilities with a clear demo moment. Weak fit: fintech (the engineered peak conflicts with trust posture), B2B (admin onboarding ≠ rating moment), social (review prompt at sign-up feels premature).
fields.tooling_options: expo-store-review (RN), react-native-store-review, native SKStoreReviewController + Google Play In-App Review API, Adapty/RevenueCat dashboards can trigger via paywall lifecycle hooks, Plotline can fire the prompt as part of an onboarding step.
fields.ai_dependency: none: the review prompt itself uses no AI. It is most powerful in concert with item 12 (AI plan reveal) which IS LLM-driven and creates the peak.
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude Design to wire the call into the onboarding state machine immediately after the reveal-complete event. Prompt fragment: 'In the OnboardingFSM, add a transition from `state: ai_reveal_complete` → `state: review_prompt_pending` → `state: paywall`. On entry to `review_prompt_pending`, call `await StoreReview.requestReview()` if `await StoreReview.isAvailableAsync()` is true, then auto-advance after 800ms regardless of user interaction (Apple's modal handles dismissal). Emit telemetry event `review_prompt_shown` with property `placement: "mid_onboarding_post_reveal"`. Forbid: showing the prompt before the AI reveal completes; showing the prompt twice; replacing the system prompt with a custom UI.'
fields.stitch_application: Prompt fragment: 'Mockup a 3-screen sequence: (Screen A) AI plan reveal headline "Here's your personalized plan" with the plan summary card. (Screen B) Native iOS Store Review prompt overlay (depict the system modal with 5 hollow stars and "Enjoying [App Name]?" headline) on top of a slightly-dimmed Screen A background. (Screen C) Paywall. Annotate that Screen B is the SYSTEM-RENDERED Apple modal, not a custom UI — designers should not redraw it. The transition A→B fires automatically 800ms after Screen A animation completes; B→C fires whether or not the user rated.'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen often (1) builds a custom 'rate us' UI instead of calling the system API — Apple rejects and the prompt fails to harvest the peak; (2) places the call too early (welcome screen) where Apple's heuristic suppresses; (3) forgets the iOS 14+ windowScene parameter and crashes; (4) calls the API every session ignoring Apple's 3/year limit (no error but wasted opportunity). Counter-prompt: 'Use SKStoreReviewController.requestReview(in: scene) on iOS 14+ — never build a custom star UI. Place the call after the AI reveal animation completes, never before. Cache `lastReviewPromptDate` in MMKV so you never call the API more than once per onboarding session.'
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Firing the prompt on first launch / welcome screen — Apple suppresses, opportunity wasted. (2) Building a custom 5-star UI to bypass Apple's rate limit — explicit App Review violation (Guideline 1.1.6). (3) Firing AFTER the paywall when negative affect from the ask sours rating valence. (4) Firing more than once per onboarding (cached). (5) 'Generic Celebration' antipattern — coupling the review prompt with mismatched archetype voice (a Caregiver app shouting 'YEAH, RATE US 5 STARS!').
fields.regulatory_risk: Low: Apple and Google review APIs are sanctioned channels. Medium edge case under EU Digital Fairness Act (Q4 2026 draft) if the prompt is paired with manipulative copy ('Rate 5 stars to unlock free trial') — that crosses into dark-pattern territory. Always use the unmodified system prompt copy. Italian AGCM has fined apps for review-incentive coercion (2024 case against ride-hailing app).
fields.platform_constraints_2026: iOS App Store Guideline 1.1.6 — apps must not 'manipulate or cheat App Store charts' which has been interpreted to include incentivized or coerced reviews. The system API is fine because Apple controls the modal. Google Play has analogous restrictions. Apple's iOS 26 retains the SKStoreReviewController API; Liquid Glass restyles the modal automatically.
fields.accessibility_implications: Apple's system review modal is fully VoiceOver-compatible by default — never wrap with a custom accessibility-broken UI. The transition into and out of the modal must respect prefers-reduced-motion (the auto-advance after 800ms should crossfade not slide). The fact that the modal is non-skippable for screen-reader users is by design — they get a 'Cancel' button.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Apple rate-limit means if the user already rated this app version on a prior install or device, the modal silently doesn't appear — your auto-advance must be timer-based, not callback-based. (2) The API gives no callback indicating whether the user actually rated — you can only infer via App Store rating count. (3) Over-aggressive placement (prompt in mid-onboarding AND mid-app) wastes Apple's 3/year quota. (4) Localization — the system modal localizes automatically to device locale.
fields.cross_cultural_validity: Practitioner reports indicate the pattern works globally, but cultural attitudes to public rating differ. JP/KR users rate apps less frequently than US users (Sensor Tower 2024 — the JP rating-to-install ratio is roughly half the US ratio). LATAM users rate enthusiastically post-positive-experience. The mid-onboarding peak strategy is locale-agnostic but absolute rating volume varies. Verify per-locale by tracking rating delta around your placement change.
fields.prescribed_action: Place the system App Store / Play Store review prompt immediately AFTER the AI plan reveal animation completes and BEFORE the paywall navigation. Use `SKStoreReviewController.requestReview(in: scene)` on iOS and `ReviewManager.launchReviewFlow()` on Android. Auto-advance to the paywall after 800ms regardless of interaction. Never build a custom star UI; never fire on cold launch.
fields.aha_relevance: Critical — this prompt is what HARVESTS the engineered Aha (item 12 AI plan reveal). The Aha generates the peak; the review prompt monetizes that peak in App Store rating equity, which compounds future install conversion via App Store presence.
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: Magician (the reveal IS the magic, the review captures that energy), Sage (validated wisdom — user just got a credible plan), Caregiver (gentle gratitude moment). Mismatch with Outlaw (rebellious archetype reads review-prompt as conformist), Jester (must use the system modal — can't make it funny).
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Pairs against 'Wasted Peak' — failing to harvest the peak emotional state. Also pairs against 'Generic Celebration' since the system modal is forced-clean, preventing tonal mismatch.
sources: [Kahneman et al. — When More Pain Is Preferred to Less: Adding a Better End (Peak-End Rule, 1993)](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-9280.1993.tb00589.x)
